Key Takeaways:
• This lesson is about creating your PLC2
• PLC2 is about the transformation – this is about the what.
• There’s generally more teaching in PLC2 than in PLC1 – dig deep and offer a tip or
strategy - go deep in teaching.
• Open with a thanks and recap of PLC1 (A lot of people that land on your PLC2 will
not have watched (or watched all of) your PLC1.
• Only spend 30-45 seconds on your recap.
• Then you need to teach valuable content.
• Again - you need to address objections – you don’t need to answer every single one
of them, but you definitely need to raise and answer the top one or two.
• Foreshadow what’s coming in PLC3.
• End your video with a call to action – give specific directions on what you’re
asking them to do.
• Make sure your video is only as long as it needs to be and not a minute longer.
• Download the PLC2 Quickstart Guide under the video for sample language to use,
tips for creating your PLC, and a PLC2 checklist.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
Now we're going through PLC2 which is the transformation. Here again is the
quick overview. It starts with a Pre-Pre, and then the PLC1, which is the opportunity, I
also like to call it the journey, PLC2, which is the transformation. That's where we're at.
Another way to think about this is PLC1 is the why, PLC2 is the what, PLC3 is the how or
the how to.
Quickly, the overview, this is about the what. This is about the transformation in
their lives, how it's going to change, and there's generally more teaching in PLC2 than in
PLC1. You want to dig deep and some type of tip or strategy here, for example, in my
PLC2 when I'm selling PLF, then the first one is about the sideways sales letter, which is
this overall structure of the launch. The second video is I go through the main three
primary types of launches, and I go deep on the Seed Launch. I go into some real deep
teaching there.
Here's an overall framework for PLC2. You want to open up with a thanks and a
recap for PLC1. You want to thank people for their comments and questions from PLC1
and give a quick recap of PLC1. The thing to remember is there's sort of two universes of
people that are going to be watching PLC2, and it's very simple. It's going to be people
that have seen PLC1 and people that haven't.
Some people, for whatever reason, they won't have watched that first video, won't
have consumed the first piece of Prelaunch content. Maybe they didn't click the email or
maybe they got there and they didn't look at it, or maybe they're starting to look at it and
they got distracted and they didn't complete it. Either way, you have to remember that.
You have to have that in the back of your mind - there are some people that are just
coming in and this is the first video.
Even if they come in and they hear you thanking people for their comments and
the questions and their actions from PLC1, even if they haven't seen PLC1, that's fine
because then that's sort of getting them thinking, "Maybe I should go check that out."
You want to give that quick recap. You don't want to spend 20 minutes doing
that. You want to spend 30 seconds or 45 seconds doing that. You want to remember,
again, there's those two universes. Even if someone has seen PLC1, they might've seen it
a few days ago so a quick recap is great. Thanking them for their comments and
questions is great because even if they didn't leave a comment of question it's building
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 7 --

4
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
the social proof. It's building the social proof and then they're thinking, "Maybe I should
comment on this one and then they'll thank me in the next time around."
One of the things you can do with this recap is you can go watch how TV does it
because they're doing such a great job. There's so much great TV now, and I'm not a huge
TV watcher, frankly, at all, but it's gotten a heck of a lot better than it used to be not that
long ago. If you look at some of the series fiction that's happening right now and you
watch how they quickly can recap maybe a whole season or two seasons in just a few
seconds, then you can certainly recap one video in a few seconds as well. That's how you
want to open this first video. Remember, this is a story arc over the three videos. It's not
like individual videos. You want to tie them together.
You want to recap the opportunity. The first video is all about that opportunity
and you want to keep on showing that opportunity. That's the bright shining star in the
future for people is that opportunity to change their lives, to have more pleasure and less
pain. Whatever it is, whatever your niche is, you're changing someone's life. You want to
keep on recapping that opportunity for their life to be better.
Don't assume they've seen that or paid attention to it or remember it from PLC1.
Even if they have, even if it's still vivid for them, it's still a great thing to revisit that
opportunity. You want to recap your positioning. You don't want to necessarily hit them
over the head really hard with this, but you always want to remind people why they
should be listening to you.
You get into the meat of it where you're doing some real teaching. In here you
could present a case study or you can just talk about someone you've worked with, or you
could talk about your results, and then you do some real teaching. You'll see this in the
example videos we've got for you from other PLF owners as well as in what I've done in
my videos. Again, you come back to crushing those objections. You want to talk about the
top two or three objections, raise them and answer them.
Similar to the way we did it in the first video, the first PLC you want to
foreshadow the next PLC. You want to let them know there's something coming and
what's going to be cool in it. You want to build anticipation for it.
The call to action, the acronym there is CTA for call to action. Again, this is
generally, you want to ask for a comment on your launch blog or maybe a share in social
media.
Some examples here. These are just some phrases you might want to think about

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
sprinkling in. Use them, definitely put them in your voice.
At the beginning, "Thanks for all the comments on that first video. It's just
awesome." This is super kinder and gentler kind of wording. It's awesome to know there
were so many kind hearted souls. I would probably, "Thanks for all the comments. It's
just awesome that there's so many people that are really interested in creating a business
that they love." Something along that lines.
In the last video I showed you, this is how you lead into that recap and how I
discovered the answer. That's also part of that recap, and what's possible. That's leading
into the vision for their future, for that opportunity for this bigger, better, greater life.
Now I want to take you deeper into this area. That's how you would transition
into some of the teaching. A lot of the people have been asking about… that's how you
lead into the objections. I've talked about here for this one and for PLC2 and for PLC1,
that we have to raise and answer objections. It isn't like you're going to say, "Hey, I know
one of the objections a lot of people have," or, "A lot of people have been objecting to this
because of this," or, "A lot of people don't believe me with this." It's just like, "A lot of
people have been asking me about this," and then you talk about the objection. "A lot of
people have been asking me about how do you launch without a list?" Then I go into the
objection, which is I don't have a list.
“A lot of people, they've told me that they've tried to grow tomatoes and they keep
on getting eaten by the rabbits. Let me tell you how I avoid that.” Frankly, I'm not a big
tomato grower so I don't know if rabbits are a problem, but I'm just making that up.
That's how you lead into objections.
“A lot of people have been asking about their fingers get sore when they try to
learn a song on their guitar. A lot of people have been asking me about when they try to
meditate, all these stray thoughts that come in their head and they don't feel like they're
doing it right.” That's how you raise those objections, really important.
In the next video, of course, you're foreshadowing the next video.
Just scroll down, leave a comment for me, tell me what you think about this. Tell
me what your number one question is about creating a meditation practice. Tell me how
I can help you grow bigger tomatoes.
When you ask for that, don't just say, "Scroll down and leave a comment," say,
"Scroll down and tell me what you're thinking about this. Scroll down and tell me what
you're first launch would mean to you. Scroll down and tell me how you would feel if you

6
© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
could hit the ball 20 yards further off the tee when you're golfing." That's how you ask for
comments. You want to direct people and tell them how to leave a comment, what to
comment about. If you do that you'll get probably three or four or five times more
comments.
Just a few tips for making your content more powerful. The video length, I sort of
talked about this at length when I was talking about PLC1. Don't ever look and say, "Boy,
so and so's videos are so long and I gotta make mine that long." You need to make them
as long as they need to be and not one second longer.
I know there are times in the past where I've had PLC3 was 50 minutes long,
which is insanely long, insanely long, but I really felt like the way I put it together it
needed to be that long. You know what? Originally it was 55 minutes long and I worked
and I worked and I worked and I worked to get it down to 50 minutes. It's not like I sit
down and say, "Boy, I want to make a 50-minute video." My PLC1's about 25 minutes or
so. It's not like I sit down and say, "Oh man, this is only 21 minutes. I've got to figure out
some way to make this four minutes longer."
What's happening is that's initially a 35 or 40-minute video, and then I cut it and
I cut it and I cut it. When I'm talking cutting I'm talking about I'll script these out. I
actually have a word for word script and I'll just keep on deleting and tightening it and
then I'll put it away and then I'll come back to it and I'll tighten it and I’ll tighten it and
I'll tighten it. If you see it as 25 minutes long, that's the final video, believe me, it started
off a lot longer. I found ways to cut it. I cut it to what I consider cut it to the bone and
couldn't make it any shorter and have it do what I wanted it to do.
You need to do that same thing. You want to make them as short as you can and
tell the complete story. Don't say, "Jeff's is 25, I'm going to make mine 25. It's only 18
now but I got to figure out some way to fill those other seven minutes." Make it as tight
as you can. As you get more and more and more experience at creating a more
compelling, tighter messaging, then you can give yourself a little more leeway and make
it a little longer, but always make it as short as you possibly can.
I've got screen capture versus full motion. A lot of people don't want to do full
motion, direct to camera we call it, talking head kind of video because they don't feel
comfortable doing it. If that's the case, then go ahead and use screen capture. For
example, what I'm doing right now is screen capture. In a lot of ways screen capture is
technically easier, but to make it really interesting, compelling PLC video, it's often
actually a little bit harder.
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

© 2017 Internet Alchemy, Inc. I www.ProductLaunchFormula.com
When people can see your face on camera it does create a more powerful
connection to you. It's generally makes you feel more real and relatable. If you can I
would go direct to camera. It tends to feel more natural and more informal and it makes
you easier to connect to. The thing is, to do that you have to be on camera. It initially can
be hard. People say, "Jeff, you're such a natural on camera." The reality is there's nothing
natural about me and a camera. It's just I've been on camera enough where it starts to
feel more natural and I get better at it. I still continue to get better at it. I'm way better
this year than I was last year.
That's one of the reasons you might have noticed on my blog, I put out a weekly
video, a weekly free video. Since I started doing that, and all yearlong I'm recording video
for that, then when it comes time to do my launch videos I'm a lot more comfortable on
camera. I've just spent 52 times in the last year being on camera, now it's time to do
Prelaunch video, - no problem.
That last bullet here, the most important thing is that you're comfortable. Given
the choice between having you do direct to camera where you're not comfortable versus
screencap where you are comfortable, do the screencap where you are comfortable.
On your launch pages you always want to link your previous Prelaunch content so
when they're watching PLC2 they can click over and see PLC3 really easily.
Another thing, this is something that we've really just started doing in the last few
years is if you add in a PDF, some type of a worksheet, a checklist, a mind map, people
love that. They absolutely love that.
Continue down. We give you this PLC2 checklist. I'm not going to go through all
this for you because you can go through it really easily. Definitely scan through here and
try to hit all these points and you'll end up with great PLC2.
